---
title: "Roofline Runner"
subtitle: "Catch kernels under the ceiling."
description: "A 30-second browser mini-game from MLSysBook Playground. Move a crosshair to catch falling kernels beneath the roofline ceiling — but not the ones above. Teaches the memory-bound vs compute-bound distinction."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
          <script src="/assets/games/common.js" defer></script>
          <script src="/assets/games/roofline.js" defer></script>
---

```{=html}
<div class="mlsp-game-container" role="region" aria-label="Roofline Runner mini-game">
  <canvas id="mlsp-canvas" class="mlsp-game-canvas" width="680" height="460"></canvas>
  <div class="mlsp-game-hud">
    <span class="mlsp-score">score <span id="mlsp-score">0</span> · <span id="mlsp-time">30</span>s left</span>
    <span>mouse / arrows · <kbd>R</kbd> retry</span>
  </div>
</div>

<div id="mlsp-aha-slot"></div>

<script>
(function mountRoofline(){
  function boot() {
    if (!window.MLSP || !MLSP.games || !MLSP.games.roofline) return setTimeout(boot, 30);
    var canvas = document.getElementById("mlsp-canvas");
    var $score = document.getElementById("mlsp-score");
    var $time  = document.getElementById("mlsp-time");
    var ahaSlot = document.getElementById("mlsp-aha-slot");
    var api = MLSP.games.roofline(canvas, {
      onScoreChange: function(s) {
        $score.textContent = s.score;
        $time.textContent  = Math.ceil(s.timeLeft / 1000);
      },
      onGameOver: function(result) {
        MLSP.showAhaCard(ahaSlot, api.ahaLabel, api.ahaText);
      },
      onRetry: function() { window.location.reload(); }
    });
  }
  boot();
})();
</script>
```

## How it works

The chart is a **roofline plot**. The horizontal axis is *arithmetic intensity* (FLOPs per byte of memory traffic), the vertical axis is achieved performance. The red line is your hardware's ceiling — on the left it's bandwidth-limited (memory-bound), on the right it flattens at peak compute. The bend in the middle is the **ridge point**.

Kernels (matmul, attention, softmax, etc.) fly in from the right at various heights. **Catch the ones below the ceiling** — those are achievable. **Don't catch the ones above** — those represent operator implementations no real hardware can sustain. Miss too many and you lose lives.

## Why this matters

Real ML systems engineering is mostly about getting useful work *under the ceiling*. A kernel might run at 5 TFLOP/s on paper but only 0.5 TFLOP/s on your GPU because it's memory-bandwidth bound — the ridge point tells you where the wall is. Spotting this in your own workloads is the difference between buying more GPUs and rewriting one kernel.

---

*Part of [MLSys Playground](/games/).*
